coredata.dc:title: Performance enhancement of triboelectric nanogenerators based on polyvinylidene fluoride/graphene quantum dot composite nanofibers
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Geon-Ju
Baek, Seong-Ho
Lee, Sang-Seok
Khan, Firoz
Kim, Jae Hyun
Park, Il-Kyu
coredata.dc:description: Wearable mechanical energy harvesting technologies have been achieved much attention for the wireless sustainable power source applications. In this study, we have fabricated polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)/graphene quantum dot (GQD) composite nanofibers (NFs), which showed improved triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) performance. PVDF/GQD composite NFs were fabricated by an electrospinning method. Structural and chemical investigations show that the GQDs were embedded in the PVDF NFs and promoted the formation of polar β-phase when an optimal amount of GQDs was incorporated. The PVDF/GQD NFs showed strong photoluminescence at a wavelength of 453 nm, which was attributed to the electronic transitions in the GQDs. As the GQD content increased from 0 to 5 vol%, the maximum output power from TENG devices increased from 35 to 97 μW but decreased with further additions of GQDs. The enhancement and degradation of the TENG performance with increasing the GQD contents were due to the enhanced formation of polar β-phase and the detrimental effect of conductive GQDs for charge trapping, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Composite
Electrospinning
Polyvinylidene fluoride
Nanofiber
Triboelectric nanogenerator

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea ( NRF-2018R1A2B6006968 ), and by the DGIST R&D Program (No. 19-NT-02) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT of the Korean government .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the fabrication process of PVDF/GQD composite NFs by the electrospinning method and diagram of the TENG device structure. Structure of N-doped GQDs and their luminescence images under UV excitation (λexc = 266 nm). The inset shows high resolution TEM images of GQDs.

FE-SEM images of the electrospun PVDF/GQD composite NFs obtained with concentrations of (a) 0 vol% (pristine), (b) 2.5 vol%, (c) 5 vol%, (d) 7.5 vol%, and (e) 10 vol%. The insets of Figs. (a), (c), and (e) show the TEM images of PVDF/GQD composite NFs with GQD contents of 0, 5, and 10 vol%, respectively. (f) Large area electrospun PVDF/GQD composite NF mat with GQD content of 5 vol%.
Normalized Raman spectra of the PVDF/GQD composite NFs with the variation of the GQD contents.

Photoluminescence spectra of the PVDF/GQD composite NFs with the variation of the GQD contents. The inset shows the energy levels of the GQDs to explain the optical behaviors of photoexcited electrons in GQDs, including their radiative recombination from sp2-related energy states.

(a) Normalized XRD patterns of the electrospun pristine PVDF NFs and PVDF/GQD composite NFs with various GQD contents. (b) Deconvoluted XRD patterns to estimate the relative amounts of α- and β-phase PVDFs. (c) Relative amount of β-phase in the PVDF NFs with the variation of the GQD contents.

(a) Normalized Raman spectra of the PVDF/GQD composite NFs with variation of the GQD contents in frequency range 750 cm−1 to 950 cm−1, revealing the α and β-phases of PVDF. (b) Variation of the β/α-phase ratio in the PVDF NFs with the variation of the GQD contents.
(a) The output voltage and (b) current of the TENGs with the variation of the GQD contents.

(a) The load resistance-dependent output power of the TENGs with the various GQD contents and the equivalent electrical circuit of the measurement system. (b) The output power of the TENGs with the variation of the GQD contents at an external load resistance of 20 MΩ. The inset is a snapshot of the commercial LED bulbs in serial-connection directly driven by the TENG with GQD contents of 5%.

head.ce:title: Performance enhancement of triboelectric nanogenerators based on polyvinylidene fluoride/graphene quantum dot composite nanofibers

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Wearable mechanical energy harvesting technologies have been achieved much attention for the wireless sustainable power source applications. In this study, we have fabricated polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF)/graphene quantum dot (GQD) composite nanofibers (NFs), which showed improved triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) performance. PVDF/GQD composite NFs were fabricated by an electrospinning method. Structural and chemical investigations show that the GQDs were embedded in the PVDF NFs and promoted the formation of polar β-phase when an optimal amount of GQDs was incorporated. The PVDF/GQD NFs showed strong photoluminescence at a wavelength of 453 nm, which was attributed to the electronic transitions in the GQDs. As the GQD content increased from 0 to 5 vol%, the maximum output power from TENG devices increased from 35 to 97 μW but decreased with further additions of GQDs. The enhancement and degradation of the TENG performance with increasing the GQD contents were due to the enhanced formation of polar β-phase and the detrimental effect of conductive GQDs for charge trapping, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, interest in environmentally friendly energy harvesting technology has been steadily growing [1–3]. The energy conversion of wasted mechanical energies such as human motion, mechanical vibration, sound, air flow, and water flow without an external power source is being actively studied. A triboelectric nanogenerator (TENG) can scavenge mechanical energy using static electricity, and mechanical energy such as small vibrations can be converted into electric energy [3–5].

Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) is a ferroelectric organic material with high electron affinity in a triboelectric series and has been widely used as an active material for TENG and polymer-based energy storage applications [6,7]. PVDF is mainly composed of α-phase, β-phase, and γ-phase. It is reported that the β-phase has the highest TENG efficiency in the poling state, in which fluorine and hydrogen are arranged side by side [8]. Because the TENG performance is affected by the contact area, the fibrous form of the materials has been investigated widely to increase the mechanical contact area between triboelectric layers [9]. For this purpose, electrospun PVDF NF structures have been attracting much attention to enhance the formation of polar β-phase and increase the effective surface area [9]. Recently, most research has focused on improving the TENG performance further by forming PVDF-based composite NFs. The TENG performance could be enhanced by adding rare-earth elements [10], carbon nanotubes [11], graphene oxide [12], metal nanoparticles [13], Ba0.6Sr0.4TiO3 [14], Fe3O4 nanoparticles [15], and silver nanowires [16].

In this paper, we report on the enhanced formation of β-phase PVDF NFs through the incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). GQDs are small-sized layered carbon nanostructures with abnormally high luminescence efficiency [17,18]. However, GQDs have many activated parts because their size is a few tens of nanometers or less with many dangling bonds on the surface. These dangling bonds can promote the phase transformation of PVDF by chemical catalytic effects. Therefore, we fabricated PVDF/GQD composite NFs and utilized them for TENG applications. In addition, the effect of the GQD content on the structural evolution of the PVDF NFs and TENG performance mechanism was investigated.

PVDF/GQD composite NFs were fabricated by an electrospinning method, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The PVDF source solution was prepared by dissolving 15 wt% PVDF powder (Kynar FLEX 2801, Arkema) in N,N-dimethylformamaide (DMF; Sigma Aldrich Korea), followed by stirring at room temperature for 24 h. The nitrogen-doped GQDs were prepared by reducing functionalized graphene oxide and polyethylenimine composite powders at 300 °C for 1 h, followed by filtration [19]. The diameter of the resulting GQDs was less than 5 nm. They were doped with nitrogen and exhibited high photoluminescence (PL) quantum efficiency above 90% [20]. The colloidal GQDs showed blue emission under UV excitation, as shown in Fig. 1. To fabricate the PVDF/GQD composite NFs, the GQDs dispersed in ethanol (1 mg/mL) were added to the PVDF solution at the volume ratio of 0 (pristine), 2.5, 5, 7.5, and 10 vol% relative to the volume of the PVDF source solution.

The PVDF solutions were loaded into 5-mL syringes with 18-gauge metal needles on an electrospinning machine (ESR200R2D, NanoNC Co.). The feed rate of the source was 1.0 mL/h, and the distance between the syringe needle and the collector was 10 cm. A working voltage of 19 kV was applied with a DC power supply. To collect the electrospun PVDF/GQD NFs, Al sheets were wrapped around the rotating drum-shaped collector, as shown in Fig. 1. The rotation speed of the collector drum was 200 rpm.

TENG devices were made from the PVDF/GQD NFs and Al metal thin films and then operated in vertical contact-separation mode, as shown in Fig. 1. The PVDF and Al are on the negative and positive sides in a triboelectric series, respectively. The Al foil used as the collector of the electrospun NF also acted as a back electrode for the PVDF substance in the TENG devices. The surfaces of the PVDF/GQD NFs and Al film (contact area: 6 × 6 cm2) can contact and separate periodically with a variable frequency and generate potential differences.

The morphology of the PVDF/GQD composite NFs was observed by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; Inspect F50) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM; HF-3300/NB5000/S-4800, Hitachi). The crystal structure was examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD; Rigaku D/MAX 2500 diffractometer equipped with a Cu Kα source). The Raman spectra were measured using a confocal Raman spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Nicolet Almeca XRA) with an excitation wavelength of 532 nm at room temperature. The PL properties of the PVDF/GQD composite NFs were characterized using a 266-nm Cr:YAG UV laser as an excitation source at room temperature. The output voltage and current of the TENGs were measured using a high-speed oscilloscope (TBS1202B, Tektronix) and picoammeter (6485, Keithley), respectively.

Fig. 2 shows FE-SEM images of the PVDF NFs with various amounts of the GQDs. The PVDF/GQD NFs showed uniform morphology regardless of the concentration of GQD and randomly oriented shape because of the spiral ejection of the NFs from the Taylor cone of the solutions. Even with increases of the GQD content, the PVDF NFs showed straight uniform shapes and no bead defects. The average diameter of the PVDF NFs was around 350–400 nm and similar for all samples. These results indicate that the surface morphologies of the PVDF NFs are not affected by the incorporation of GQDs.

The TEM images for the pristine PVDF NFs showed uniform contrast (inset of Fig. 2(a)), while those of the NFs with GQD incorporated showed dark spots, which increased with increasing GQD contents, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(c) and (e). When a small amount of GQDs was added (less than 5 vol%), they were individually distributed, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(c). However, as the GQD content increased to more up to 10 vol%, the GQDs were agglomerated, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2(e). This is due to aggregation between GQD sheets in the DMF-based PVDF solutions because the DMF solvent provides more polar conditions for the GQD particles than ethanol. These results indicate that the GQDs were successfully incorporated and dispersed in the PVDF NFs when an optimal amount was added. Fig. 2(f) shows the as-prepared electrospun PVDF/GQD composite NF mats, which show successful fabrication with a large area. Even with the incorporation of GQDs, the color of the PVDF NF mats was not changed.

Raman spectroscopy was done to verify the incorporation of GQDs in the PVDF NFs. The Raman spectra of the pristine PVDF NFs showed no peak in this range, as shown in Fig. 3 . As the GQD content increased, peaks started to appear around ∼1405 and ∼1580 cm−1, and their intensity gradually increased. These two peaks corresponding to the defect related peak (D peak, ∼1405 cm−1) and the hexagonal lattice of graphene associated with the double-degenerated E2g mode (G peaks, ∼1580 cm−1) [20]. Graphene usually shows D peaks at 1350 cm−1 and originate from the breathing mode of six-atom rings. This mode is activated by an intravalley scattering process and requires a defect for its activation [20]. In our case, however, the D peaks shifted to larger wavenumbers by 55 cm−1 from the reported value of 1350 cm−1, which is due to N-doping and biaxial strain. The vibrational energy is strongly affected by the biaxial strain, and the up-shift indicates that the materials are under tensile stress in the matrix [21]. This suggests that the GQDs are influenced by a significant amount of compressive strain since they are embedded in the PVDF NFs, which experience shrinkage during the evaporation of the solvent. The Raman spectroscopy also confirmed that the GQDs were successfully incorporated in the PVDF NFs and that their content increases.

To investigate the optical properties of the GQDs in the PVDF matrix, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured at room temperature. The colloidal GQDs showed broad blue PL emission with a peak at 453 nm (2.74 eV), as shown in Figs. 1 and 4 . The PL emission is attributed to the electronic transitions in the GQDs. As shown in the energy levels of the GQDs, the absorption of the excitation wavelength (266 nm) is associated with the Cπ → Cπ* transition. The PL emissions are attributed to the Cπ* → Cπ, Nπ*→ Cπ, and Oπ*→ Cπ transitions in the N-doped GQDs [18,19]. These various possible transitions for the emission resulted in broad emission spectra as shown in Fig. 4.

The pristine PVDF NFs showed no emission, while the PVDF/GQD composite NFs showed strong PL emission peaks at 453 nm, which is similar with that of colloidal GQDs. The PL emission intensity increased as the GQD content increased from 2.5 to 10 vol%. These observations imply that the GQDs acted as a single domain even though they were slightly agglomerated, which was shown in the TEM results.

XRD analysis was performed on the PVDF/GQD composite NFs to further investigate the evolution of the crystalline structure of PVDF NFs with increasing GQD concentration. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), the pristine and composite NFs mainly showed two broad peaks at 2θ = 18° and 20° without any trace of additional phases corresponding to impurities and precipitates. The peak near 2θ = 18° is composed of three peaks for the (100) and (020) planes of α-phase and the (020) plane of γ-phase. The peak near 2θ = 20° is composed of four peaks for the (110) plane of α-phase, the (110) plane of γ-phase, and the (200) and (110) planes of β-phase [8,22]. As the GQD content increases, the peak at 20° shifted to a higher angle, which indicates that the β-phase formation was enhanced, while the peak corresponding to the α- and γ-phases near 18° decreased.

To further investigate the evolution of phases in the PVDF NFs, the elementary spectra were deconvoluted, and the relative amounts of α- and β-phase PVDFs was estimated, as shown in Fig. 5(b). Fig. 5(c) shows the estimated relative amount of β-phase in the PVDF NFs with the variation of the GQD contents. The β-phase/total ratio of the pristine PVDF NFs was about 0.1, which increased to 0.15 as the GQD content increased up to 5 vol%. This observation strongly indicates that the GQD addition plays a critical role in enhancing the formation of β-phase in PVDF NFs. This was confirmed by the Raman spectra of PVDF. Fig. 6 (a) shows three major Raman modes at 798, 839, and 881 cm−1, which represent the presence of the α-phase, β-phase, and all phases (α, β, and γ-phases), respectively [23]. The Raman spectra shows that the relative amount of the β-phase increased compared to that of the α-phase with increasing the GQD contents. Fig. 6(b) shows the variation of β/α-phase ratio in the PVDF NFs with the variation of the GQD contents. The β/α-phase ratio increased from 0.66 to 1.12 as the GQD content increased from 0 to 5 vol%. Further increase of the GQD contents up to 10 vol% resulted in the decrease of the β/α-phase ratio down to 1.07. These results are consistent with the observations verified by the XRD measurement.

The GQDs in this study were negatively charged due to the N-doping and the amine-groups are present on their surfaces, as shown schematically in Fig. 1 [19]. The negatively charged GQD surface forms an ion-dipole interaction with the positive CH2 dipoles in the PVDF chains and stimulates the formation of the β-phase in PVDF NFs [24]. As the GQD content increased up to 10 vol%, the β-phase ratio gradually decreased to 0.11. With increasing content of the GQDs, they agglomerated as shown in the TEM results, which prevent the directional dipole alignment of the PVDF chains.

The structural and optical investigations of the PVDF/GQD composite NFs clearly showed that the GQDs were successfully incorporated into the PVDF NFs, and they promoted the formation of polar PVDF phases. This approach is suitable for enhancing the TENG performance. To this end, we demonstrated TENG devices based on PVDF/GQD NF composites with a single electrode. TENGs were operated in vertical contact-separation mode, which generates electrical energy through the charge transfer between the PVDF/GQD NF and Al electrode surfaces. As their surfaces contact each other, the electrical charges are transferred from Al to PVDF NFs because the PVDF is more negative than Al in triboelectric series. Also, when the two surfaces are separated from each other, the charge flows into the circuit through the electrodes while maintaining electrical neutrality. As shown in Fig. 7 , downward and upward voltage and current signals were generated during contact and separation of the two surfaces. As the GQD content increased from 0 to 5 vol%, the output voltage and current increased consistently. Compared with the pristine PVDF NFs, the voltage and current outputs of the PVDF NFs containing 5 vol% GQDs were enhanced by 2 times and 1.5 times, respectively. However, as the GQD content increased further up to 7.5 and 10 vol%, the voltage and current outputs were drastically reduced.

To further investigate the TENG performance, we measured the voltage and current outputs with respect to the load resistance by connecting load resistances varying from 11.79 kΩ to 1 GΩ. The output of electrical power was calculated using the electrical power equation, P = IV. The current and voltage values were the instantaneous peak values. The TENG devices showed increased output power at first, and then it decreased again with the increasing load resistance. Most devices showed maximum power at around R
Load ∼20 MΩ, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). These observations show similar behavior to those of many other reported TENG devices [25,26]. This confirms that the TENG devices based on the PVDF/GQD composite NFs operate normally.

We compared the maximum output power with the variation of the load resistance, as shown in Fig. 8(b). As the GQD content increased from 0 to 5 vol%, the maximum output power increased from 35 to 97 μW. Further additions of GQDs beyond 5 vol% resulted in decreased output power below 5 μW. This result was consistent with the variation of the β-phase ratio in the PVDF NFs shown in Fig. 5(c). The enhanced formation of the β-phase crystals in the PVDF NFs due to GQD incorporation shifts the surface charge potential of the PVDF NF surface to become more negative. A more negative surface charge potential at the PVDF NF surfaces induces increased potential difference with the top Al electrode, which improves the TENG performance. Thus, the TENG performance directly follows the variation of the β-phase fraction in the PVDF NFs. In addition, conductive GQD sheets embedded in dielectric PVDF NFs can act as charge trapping and storing states [16]. As the PVDF and Al surfaces become close to each other, the charges should be transferred from the Al to PVDF. However, if the small conductive GQDs are embedded in the PVDF, electrical charges can be also generated between the PVDF and GQD interfaces. This induces a screening effect for the electrical potential formation and results in the degradation of the TENG performance as large amounts of GQDs are incorporated. Therefore, these two combined detrimental effects of reduced β-phase fraction and charge screening effect further impede the enhancement of the TENG performance. To this end, we demonstrated the powering system for commercial light-emitting diode (LED) bulbs by connecting them directly to the TENG device (GQD 5 vol%) without any energy storage device like a capacitor. Nine red, green, and blue LEDs connected in series were turned on simultaneously as shown in the inset of Fig. 8(b). They showed bright emission even in the room light. This demonstration suggested that the composite NFs have potential as sustainable power sources for driving wearable smart electronics. We successfully fabricated PVDF/GQD composite NFs and the optimal amount of GQD incorporation stimulated the polar β-phase formation and enhanced the TENG performance. In addition, the fabricated PVDF/GQD composite NFs showed strong light emission, which could be applicable for self-powered triboelectric electroluminescent smart textiles [27].

In summary, PVDF/GQD composite NFs were fabricated by using an electrospinning method. Structural investigations showed that the GQDs were successfully incorporated into the PVDF NFs, and they promoted the formation of polar β-phase because the negatively charged GQD surface forms an ion-dipole interaction with the positive CH2 dipoles in the PVDF. As the GQD content increased from 0 to 5 vol%, the maximum output power of TENG devices based on the PVDF/GQD composite NFs increased from 35 to 97 μW, while they decreased with the further addition of GQDs beyond 5 vol%. The enhancement and degradation of the TENG performance with increasing GQD contents originated from the enhanced formation of polar β-phase and detrimental effects of conductive GQDs for charge trapping. Therefore, the optimal amount of GQD incorporation stimulated the polar β-phase formation and enhanced the TENG performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF-2018R1A2B6006968), and by the DGIST R&D Program (No. 19-NT-02) funded by the Ministry of Science and ICT of the Korean government.
